text: So this would seem a rather straightforward laparoscopic high anterior resection for a locally advanced distal sigmoid proximal rectal cancer. But what made this case a bit more challenging are the adhesions from his previous laparotomy.
The surgical team stands on the left side of the patient, carefully dissecting and removing adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall to allow for safe placement of additional laparoscopic ports.

Another challenging aspect about this case really is that this guy has a long and loopy sigmoid colon as you can see in the CAT scan. that's fairly stuck to the right side. This has to be straightened up before you can actually begin with the standard steps of the operation.
The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to carefully dissect and separate adhesions within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon moves to the right side of the patient to begin the neutral to lateral resection, positioning for the next phase of the abdominoperineal resection procedure.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum widely, aiming to locate the inferior mesenteric pedicle package within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon inspects the duodenum, marking the cephalic extent of the dissection. A sizable lymph node is visible in the pyramus enteric pedicle, which needs to be excised en bloc.
The surgeon exposes the smooth and shiny surface of the inferior mesenteric pedicle package, proceeding with dissection just behind this pedicle.
The surgeon approaches the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, carefully skeletonizing the vessel to prepare for the application of hemo locks.
The surgeon identifies a sizable lymph node in the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) chain and prepares to remove it with appropriate margins, ensuring complete excision.
The surgeon completes the skeletonization of the inferior mesenteric artery down to the adventitia level, applying hemolux clips to secure the vessel before transecting it at a high point.
The surgeon grasps the cut end of the germicenteric pedicle to facilitate the medial to lateral dissection, focusing on the exposed tissue planes.

The surgeon begins pelvic dissection, identifying the inferior hypogastric nerve in the area. The dissection aims to preserve the nerve while following the delicate nerve fibers, known as 'angel's hair,' into the deep pelvis.
The surgeon performs posterior dissection, meticulously following the areolar tissues while gently pushing the visceretum upwards to maintain clear separation from surrounding structures.

The surgeon exposes a distinct dissection plane by cutting on the yellow side of the white tissue, utilizing a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to maintain the vertebral structures in their correct anatomical position.
The surgeon places a gloss for image relevance and proceeds to the lateral side, where adhesions are carefully dissected to expose the left gutter.
The surgeon continues the dissection on the lateral side, approaching the urinary bladder. The previously encountered challenges appear minimal as most of the dissection has already been completed.

The surgeon returns to the pelvic region, carefully unzipping the peritoneum while ensuring the left ureter remains unharmed. Dissection proceeds close to the mesorectum to avoid damaging nerves located in the lateral sidewalls.

The surgeon continues the posterior dissection, moving to the lateral side while following the areolar tissue planes. The area near the tumor is dissected, revealing what appears to be a portion of the urinary bladder.
The surgeon uses a colonoscope to accurately localize the tumor within the colon, followed by distal dissection to achieve the desired transaction margin.
The surgeon measures the distal transection point at five centimeters and cuts perpendicularly across it, ensuring precise alignment for the resection.
The surgeon focuses on the pyramus enteric vein, located just below the inferior border of the pancreas, carefully skeletonizing it to ensure no other vessels are present alongside it.
The surgeon trims the mesentery to facilitate extraction through the fan-shaped incisions, ensuring the mesentery is adequately separated for removal.
The surgeon uses an open stapler to transect the distal margin in a single maneuver.
The surgeon completes the colorectal anastomosis using standard techniques, followed by performing the air leak test to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the anastomotic integrity using a colonoscope, visually inspecting the connection for any signs of leakage or irregularities.
